does: This paper introduces Asymmetric Langevin Unlearning (ALU), a certified unlearning framework that leverages public data to reduce the noise required for privacy guarantees while preserving model utility. The core contribution is proving that injecting public data suppresses the unlearning cost by a factor of O(1/n_pub²), enabling practitioners to trade increased public data volume for reduced noise injection and improved model performance. The authors analyze both the ideal case and realistic distribution mismatch scenarios, characterizing how shifts between public and private data sources affect utility degradation. Empirically, ALU demonstrates the ability to perform mass unlearning of large dataset fractions—a regime where standard symmetric noise-based methods completely fail—while maintaining computational advantage over full retraining across various scales and settings.
connects: This work extends the intersection of differential privacy and machine unlearning, building on the established noise-based certification approaches that guarantee unlearning through Langevin dynamics and similar mechanisms. It directly incorporates insights from differential privacy literature where public data amplification has long been a technique to reduce privacy costs and improve utility-privacy trade-offs. The paper responds to the fundamental limitation in certified unlearning that noise magnitude requirements destroy utility for large-scale deletions, a tension recognized but not systematically addressed in prior unlearning work. By introducing asymmetry between public and private data sources, it bridges techniques from privacy-amplification literature into the unlearning domain, extending beyond prior symmetric approaches that treat all data equally.
enables: This framework opens several research directions in practical certified unlearning systems. The explicit analysis of distribution mismatch provides tools for understanding how mismatched public data affects unlearning guarantees, enabling future work on acquiring or synthesizing better-matched public datasets. The demonstrated capability to unlearn constant fractions of large datasets suggests applications to realistic deletion scenarios like GDPR compliance at scale. Future work could explore optimal public data procurement strategies, combinations with other utility-preserving techniques like low-rank adaptation, and extensions to heterogeneous deletion patterns where different samples contribute differently to unlearning cost. The framework also positions public data as a strategic resource in unlearning system design, potentially spurring research into synthetic or domain-specific public data generation for unlearning applications.
